text: Sounds interesting! I'm looking forward to reading through those papers.
Thanks! Happy to discuss them with you
Yes, we may have more to learn from the art and design world than the engineering world. I used to think of software as engineering, but now wonder if its closer to industrial design.
Yes, we may have more to learn from the art and design world than the engineering world. I used to think of software as engineering, but now wonder if its closer to industrial design.

I circle around this a lot. My current wishy-washy resolution is that there are many different kinds of software. Engineering may be a useful metaphor for techniques to build infrastructure software, while software with users might be closer to industrial design or art or writing

I circle around this a lot. My current wishy-washy resolution is that there are many different kinds of software. Engineering may be a useful metaphor for techniques to build infrastructure software, while software with users might be closer to industrial design or art or writing
In my day-to-day work and usage of software, I see lots of *spaces* and *places*, both in code and in the software that code generates, so I think the architecture and building metaphors apply.
In my day-to-day work and usage of software, I see lots of *spaces* and *places*, both in code and in the software that code generates, so I think the architecture and building metaphors apply.
Re comparing code to engineering, <@UE1JQM9HQ> has a good counter argument. Software is constantly changing unlike a bridge which is built once. Coding is about constantly making decisions, adapting

I agree with <@UDYGH7K8V>, there is no one analogy. Some software is like a machine, some like a tool, some like a book, some like a bridge, some like a policy or procedure.
I agree with <@UDYGH7K8V>, there is no one analogy. Some software is like a machine, some like a tool, some like a book, some like a bridge, some like a policy or procedure.
There's a lot of friction in business/industry when people buy software that is sold like a machine, but it's actually just an encoding of their business processes and policies
There's a lot of friction in business/industry when people buy software that is sold like a machine, but it's actually just an encoding of their business processes and policies
Obviously this kind of software can never be "finished" in the same way a business is never "finished"
Obviously this kind of software can never be "finished" in the same way a business is never "finished"

Nice work on the AST visual exploration tool! I like how you use edges to denote the nodes in the AST. I also find interesting the interaction (I supposed is dragging horizontally) you use for controlling the AST detail level. A few questions:
- What do colors represent? Are they types of AST nodes?
- What layout do you use? In particular, why do edges overlap given that you have a tree?

Nice work on the AST visual exploration tool! I like how you use edges to denote the nodes in the AST. I also find interesting the interaction (I supposed is dragging horizontally) you use for controlling the AST detail level. A few questions:

- What layout do you use? In particular, why do edges overlap given that you have a tree?

I can’t remember if I’ve said this in this slack or not but:
While I think engineering metaphors are not great for most user facing software, or for the practices of building that software, I _do_ think its valuable politically to claim the mantle of being an engineer. There’s a lot of cultural capital in engineering that `design` doesn’t have. I think its worth being aware as you theorize what the implications of that theory would be :slightly_smiling_face: With <@UDQBTJ211>’s example, businesses are (often) reluctant to give up control over their practices. While the software you’re building _is_ encoding business practices, that might be a tougher sell than buying a machine.

Hey Tudor
Hey Tudor

The colors are indeed types of AST nodes, I've put 0 thought into how they are colored, the color is simply

`"#" + intToRGB(hashCode(this.node.type))`

The colors are indeed types of AST nodes, I've put 0 thought into how they are colored, the color is simply

`"#" + intToRGB(hashCode(this.node.type))`

I'm also not using an intelligent layout, edges overlap because I'm using a homebrew, very, very simple recursive rendering algorithm. It's what you would use to render a binary tree, but a varying amount of child-branches instead of 2.
The only thing I added was that if there are more than 10 branches, then I wrap all of them around in a circle, I chose this randomly, it worked fairly well to pin down hotspots

I'm also not using an intelligent layout, edges overlap because I'm using a homebrew, very, very simple recursive rendering algorithm. It's what you would use to render a binary tree, but a varying amount of child-branches instead of 2.
The only thing I added was that if there are more than 10 branches, then I wrap all of them around in a circle, I chose this randomly, it worked fairly well to pin down hotspots
The visualisation itself is very rudimentary and nothing more than a proof-of-concept

I'm experimenting with using gosper curves (as per this paper, gosper maps: <https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/6532285>)
to allow for indefinite nesting of hierarchical structures

attachments.title: GosperMap: Using a Gosper Curve for Laying Out Hierarchical Data - IEEE Journals & Magazine
attachments.text: The emergence of very large hierarchies that result from the increase in available data raises many problems of visualization and navigation. On data sets

I like thinking of writing software as a subset of writing in general.

Both are means of communication. Both can be used for something quick and dirty — "Get Milk" on a post-it or `User.where(expired:true).count` in a REPL. Both are used to make great, vast works that are intended to touch huge swathes of humanity and last for a very long time.

There are differences in how they function as tools, and the kinds of mindset and coordination needed. Writing is often personal, software is often not.

Writing is often done in a medium meant for continual evolution or advancement — I love my whiteboard with layers of caked-on text atop layers of caked-on text. You could argue that Chat is the CI of writing. You could argue that an annual OS update is akin to an annual textbook or encyclopedia revision.

Rich Hickey, in his 2 year hammock time, might just as well have written a novel. To an outside observer, the process might have looked identical.

I like the goal you set. I also think it would be worthwhile to pick one issue at a time, and ideally also pick a specific problem that you would like to solve.
I like the goal you set. I also think it would be worthwhile to pick one issue at a time, and ideally also pick a specific problem that you would like to solve.

Exploration is always interesting, but without focusing on a specific problem, the risk is that it remains only interesting without becoming useful. Focusing on a problem instead helps you guide your design decisions.

Exploration is always interesting, but without focusing on a specific problem, the risk is that it remains only interesting without becoming useful. Focusing on a problem instead helps you guide your design decisions.

It isn't hard to prove that JavaScript is about 99% a perfect copy of AS2, because you can write a program in AS3, run it through a type-stripper find'replace script and voila you have a JavaScript program. Nowadays with the ES2015 and later additions to JS they have crept towards AS3 which although it has types so it is more like JS of the future does show the shameless copying that the JS team has done of the AS language. I just converted 25,000 lines of code the other day from AS3 to JS. You can tell it is a copy because of the strange quirks in AS which were copied verbatim. For example, when you ask the date functions for the month of their in AS2/3 it returns the month based on 0, but the day based on 1. The JS date functions do this identically. And the Math... library is almost identical. When you have worked with converting AS3 code (originating from Adobe's Flash Builder, you can see how close they really are. They can't admit they copied it for legal reasons, so the myth that magically a fully formed syntax emerged in a mere 2 weeks persists. Larry Ellison once he bought Sun spent serious money suing Google over how Java was used, and to avoid another incident like that nobody wants to bring up the subject. Frankly Adobe is a much nicer company than Sun, and  I doubt they would ever be so litigious as Oracle is. What annoys me is how poorly they have copied the AS3 features like Modules; the ES Module system is deeply flawed because the implementors have misread the specification on modules, and if you have a module like Math, and import all the symbols, using import * from Math, and refer to a function in Math that doesn't exist, the compiler doesn't catch it. Oops! that is pretty dumb. The current JS compilers think of a module name as an object with an undefined property.  Once again people have neglected to study Prof. Wirth's brilliant work with Modula-2.
I have some info on Beads at <http://e-dejong.com|e-dejong.com>, but there is no GitHub repository yet.
